elements.sent-2411-001: Machine unlearning algorithms are increasingly important as legal concerns arise around the provenance of training data, but verifying the success of unlearning is often difficult.
elements.sent-2411-002: Provable guarantees for unlearning are often limited to supervised learning settings.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we provide the first theoretical guarantees for unlearning in the pre-training and fine-tuning paradigm by studying topic models, simple bag-of-words language models that can be adapted to solve downstream tasks like retrieval and classification.
elements.sent-2411-004: First, we design a provably effective unlearning algorithm for topic models that incurs a computational overhead independent of the size of the original dataset.
elements.sent-2411-005: Our analysis additionally quantifies the deletion capacity of the model -- i.e., the number of examples that can be unlearned without incurring a significant cost in model performance.
elements.sent-2411-006: Finally, we formally extend our analyses to account for adaptation to a given downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-007: In particular, we design an efficient algorithm to perform unlearning after fine-tuning the topic model via a linear head.
elements.sent-2411-008: Notably, we show that it is easier to unlearn pre-training data from models that have been fine-tuned to a particular task, and one can unlearn this data without modifying the base model.
elements.sent-2411-009: Modern-day machine learning has shifted from single-stage supervised learning on manually constructed datasets to a paradigm in which models are pre-trained and subsequently fine-tuned~\citep{bommasani2022opportunitiesrisksfoundationmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this setting, a model initially learns a good representation of the data using a self-supervised objective on a large unstructured corpus.
elements.sent-2411-011: The resulting pre-trained model is later adapted to solve specific tasks for which it is difficult or costly to curate a large dataset.
elements.sent-2411-012: This blueprint has yielded strong performance in text~\citep[e.g.,][]{devlin2019bert,brown2020language , vision~\citep[e.g.,][]{oquab2024dinov,he2022masked , and multimodal~\citep[e.g.,][]{radford2021learning,zhai2023sigmoid settings.}}}

elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \label{algo: high level} \caption{High level learning algorithm ($\mathcal{A}$)} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: document corpus $S =\{d_i\}_{i=1}^m$, anchor word tolerance $\epsilon_0$ \STATE Output: matrices $\mA$,$\mR$ \STATE $\mQ$ = word co-occurrences \STATE $\Bar{\mQ}$ = row-normalized $\mQ$ \STATE $P$ = RecoverAnchors($\{\Bar{\mQ}_1,\dots, \Bar{\mQ}_n\}$) \STATE $\mA, \mR$ = RecoverTopics($\mQ, S$) \RETURN $\mA, \mR$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-004: [H] \caption{RecoverAnchors, same as \citet{arora2012practicalalgorithmtopicmodeling}} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Row-normalized co-ocurrence matrix $\Bar{\mQ}$ and $\epsilon_0$ tolerance parameter \STATE Output: $r$ points of this perturbed simplex close to the vertices of the actual simplex \STATE Project the rows to a randomly chosen $4\log n /\eps_0^2$ dimensional subspace \STATE $S\leftarrow \{\bar \mQ_i\}$ where $\bar\mQ_i$ is the furthest point from the origin \FOR{$i$ in $1,\dots,r-1$} \STATE Let $\bar \mQ_j$ be the row of $\mQ$ with largest distance to span($S$) \STATE $S\leftarrow S\cup \{\bar\mQ_j\}$ \ENDFOR $S=\{\bar \mQ_{s_1},\dots, \bar \mQ_{s_r}\}$ \FOR {$i$ in $1,\dots, r$} \STATE Let $\bar\mQ_j$ be the point that has largest distance to span($S\setminus \{\bar \mQ_{s_i}\}$) \STATE Remove $\bar \mQ_{s_i}$ from $S$ and insert $\bar\mQ_j$ into $S$ \ENDFOR \RETURN S \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-005: [H] \caption{Recover Topics, from \citet{arora2012practicalalgorithmtopicmodeling} } \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Co-ocurrence matrix $\mQ$, anchor words $P = \{s_1,\dots, s_k\}$, tolerance parameter $\epsilon_0$ \STATE Output: Matrices $\mA,\mR$ \STATE $\Bar{\mQ}$ = row normalized $\mQ$ \STATE Store the normalization constants $\vp = \mQ\mathbf{1}$ \FOR{$i$ in $1,\dots, n$} \STATE Solve $\mC_i = \argmin\limits_{\vv\in \Delta_r} \|\Bar{\mQ}_i - \vv^\top \Bar{\mQ}_P) \|^2$ \STATE up to $\eps_0$ accuracy \ENDFOR \STATE $\mA^\prime = \mathrm{diag}(\vp) \mC$ \STATE $\mA$ = column-sum-one normalized $\mA^\prime$ \STATE $\mR= \mA^\dagger \mQ \mA^{\dagger\top}$ where $\mA^\dagger$ is the pseudoinverse of $\mA$ \RETURN $\mA,\mR$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.algorithm-2411-007: [H] \caption{Recover Topics, from \citet{arora2012practicalalgorithmtopicmodeling} } \begin{algorithmic} \STATE Input: Co-ocurrence matrix $\mQ$, anchor words $P = \{s_1,\dots, s_k\}$, tolerance parameter $\epsilon_0$ \STATE Output: Matrices $\mA,\mR$ \STATE $\Bar{\mQ}$ = row normalized $\mQ$ \STATE Store the normalization constants $\vp = \mQ\mathbf{1}$ \FOR{$i$ in $1,\dots, n$} \STATE Solve $\mC_i = \argmin\limits_{\vv\in \Delta_r} \|\Bar{\mQ}_i - \vv^\top \Bar{\mQ}_P \|^2$ \STATE up to $\eps_0$ accuracy \ENDFOR \STATE $\mA^\prime = \mathrm{diag}(\vp) \mC$ \STATE $\mA$ = column-sum-one normalized $\mA^\prime$ \STATE $\mR= \mA^\dagger \mQ \mA^{\dagger\top}$ where $\mA^\dagger$ is the pseudoinverse of $\mA$ \RETURN $\mA,\mR$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-408: First, the following lemma to bound $\mA^F \vw_\tau^F - \mA^\star \vw^\star$.
elements.sent-2411-409: We have that \begin{equation}\|\mA^F \vw_\tau^F - \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_2 \leq O\qty(B\sqrt{nr} \frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p} + \sqrt{r\tau})\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-410: We decompose as follows. \begin{equation}\mA^F \vw_\tau^F - \mA^\star \vw^\star = (\mA^F \vw_\tau^F - \mA^F \vw^F) + (\mA^F \vw^F - \mA^F \vw^\star) + (\mA^F \vw^\star - \mA^\star \vw^\star)\end{equation} The second term is bounded by \begin{equation}\|\mA^F \vw^F - \mA^F \vw^\star\|_2 \leq \sqrt{r}\|\vw^F - \vw^\star\|_2 \leq O(\sqrt{r}\|\mA^F - \mA^\star\|_\infty) \leq O\qty(\sqrt{r} \frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p})\end{equation} The third term is bounded by\begin{equation}\|\mA^F \vw^\star - \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_2 \leq O\qty(\frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0\gamma p} \sqrt{nr} B)\end{equation} by considering the spectral norm $\|\mA^F - \mA^\star\|_2$.
elements.sent-2411-411: Finally, the first term is bounded, from an above argument, by $\sqrt{2r\tau/\lambda}$.

elements.sent-2411-413: It holds that \begin{equation}\|(\mA^S)^\dagger \Bar{\mA}\Bar{\vw_\tau} - (\mA^S)^\dagger \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_2 \leq \qty(\frac{1}{p}\cdot \qty[ \sqrt{r} \qty(\frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p})^2 + B\sqrt{nr} \frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p} + \sqrt{r\tau} + \sqrt{r}\tau])\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-414: The calculation is as follows. \begin{equation}\E\qty[\|\tilde{\vv} - (\mA^S)^\dagger \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_\infty] \leq \E\qty[\|\nu_\vv\|_\infty] + \E\qty[\|(\mA^S)^\dagger \Bar{\mA} \Bar{\vw_\tau} - (\mA^S)^\dagger \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_\infty] \leq \qty(\frac{1}{p}\cdot \qty[ \sqrt{r} \qty(\frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p})^2 + B\sqrt{nr} \frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p} + \sqrt{r\tau} + \sqrt{r}\tau] ) \qty(\frac{\sqrt{\log r \log 1/\delta}}{\eps} + 1)\end{equation} For this to be a small constant, we require \begin{equation}\frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p} \leq \tilde{O}\qty(\min\left \{\frac{1}{r^{1/4}}, \frac{1}{\sqrt{nr}} \right\})\end{equation} since we already have $\tau=O(1/r)$ by assumption.
elements.formula-2411-108: \begin{equation}\E\qty[\|\tilde{\vv} - (\mA^S)^\dagger \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_\infty] \leq \E\qty[\|\nu_\vv\|_\infty] + \E\qty[\|(\mA^S)^\dagger \Bar{\mA} \Bar{\vw_\tau} - (\mA^S)^\dagger \mA^\star \vw^\star\|_\infty] \leq \qty(\frac{1}{p}\cdot \qty[ \sqrt{r} \qty(\frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p})^2 + B\sqrt{nr} \frac{(ar)^2 m_U}{m\eps_0 \gamma p} + \sqrt{r\tau} + \sqrt{r}\tau] ) \qty(\frac{\sqrt{\log r \log 1/\delta}}{\eps} + 1)\end{equation}
